1: A theme: the superscription connects Ps 3 to David fleeing Absalom — a son's rebellion driving out the father. This historical situation becomes a type of the deeper exile Christ entered: the one who came to his own and was not received (John 1:11), who was handed over by those he came to save. The many enemies of Ps 3 who say 'there is no salvation for him in God' (v2) anticipate the mockers at the cross ('He trusts in God; let God deliver him now,' Matthew 27:43).
2: A theme: 'many are saying of my soul, there is no salvation for him in God.' The mocking of the sufferer's trust in YHWH is the exact taunting at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:43: 'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him'). The psalm provides the script that the mockers at Golgotha follow unknowingly — and which the resurrection answers definitively. There was salvation for him in God, precisely because he bore the weight of those who had none.
3: A revelation of God: 'but you, O LORD, are a shield about me, my glory, and the lifter of my head.' The divine protection that David confesses in extremity is what Christ claimed in the Garden of Gethsemane: 'Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will' (Matthew 26:39). The Father who is the shield of his anointed did not ultimately abandon the Son — the resurrection is the lifting of the head after the crucifixion's shame.
4: A revelation of God: 'I cried aloud to the LORD, and he answered me from his holy hill.' The confident prayer answered from Zion — the divine responsiveness to the royal sufferer. The hill of Zion from which YHWH answers anticipates the resurrection-proclamation from Jerusalem: YHWH answered the crucified Son from the 'holy hill' by raising him on the third day (Acts 2:24).
5: A shadow: 'I lay down and slept; I woke again, for the LORD sustained me.' The sleep-and-waking of the endangered person becomes a type of death and resurrection when applied to Christ. Jesus quoted similar language of trust in the Father — and slept peacefully in the storm-tossed boat (Mark 4:38) as one who trusted the Father's sustaining power over death itself. The resurrection is the ultimate waking that YHWH's sustaining makes possible.
6: A revelation of God: 'I will not be afraid of many thousands of people who have set themselves against me all around.' The fearlessness of the anointed sufferer surrounded by enemies — Christ in Gethsemane, at the arrest, at the trial, on the cross — surrounded by 'many thousands' who sought his destruction, and yet trusting absolutely in the Father's rescue. The steadiness that Psalms 3:6 describes is what the Gospel narratives portray as Jesus's unshaken calm before Pilate and on the cross.
7: A theme: 'arise, O LORD! Save me, O my God! For you strike all my enemies on the cheek; you break the teeth of the wicked.' The petition for divine arising echoes the resurrection-prayer: 'arise' is the language of YHWH's redemptive action throughout the Psalter and prophets. The striking of enemies on the cheek is ironic when applied to Christ — he was the one struck on the cheek (John 18:22; Matthew 5:39: 'if anyone slaps you on the right cheek, turn to him the other also'). The one who absorbed the blow becomes, in resurrection, the one before whom all enemies are struck down.
8: A theme: 'salvation belongs to the LORD; your blessing be on your people!' The psalm's closing declaration — salvation (yeshu'ah) is YHWH's possession and gift — is the theological affirmation that the name Jesus embodies (Matthew 1:21: 'you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins'). Salvation belongs to YHWH — and YHWH has given it a human name and face in the Son.
